narratives.title: The geography of mental health need
narratives.body: Across London’s 4,994 neighbourhoods, mental health burden is profoundly unequal. The average SAMHI index score is -0.28, but this masks a range from -2.0 to 4.7. The boroughs bearing the heaviest burden — Havering, Sutton, Hackney — score significantly above the London mean, while City of London, Newham, Redbridge fare comparatively better.

narratives.title: The deprivation–mental health nexus
narratives.body: IMD deprivation and mental health need are strongly correlated (r = 0.37). Neighbourhoods in the most deprived SAMHI decile have an average antidepressant prescribing rate of 28.7 per 1,000, compared to 22.5 in the least deprived areas. Employment rates show an inverse pattern (r = -0.21 with SAMHI), suggesting economic inactivity and mental health distress reinforce each other.

narratives.title: The pandemic’s lasting shadow
narratives.body: Comparing 2019 to 2022, every London borough saw its SAMHI index worsen. The boroughs hit hardest were Bexley (+0.37), Havering (+0.28), and Harrow (+0.27). These are not areas that ‘bounced back’ — the data suggests a structural deepening of mental health need post-COVID.

narratives.title: Scale of need
narratives.body: An estimated 103,910 adults (aged 16+) live in neighbourhoods ranked in the top 30% for mental health need (SAMHI deciles 8–10). That represents 1% of London’s adult population. The average GP depression prevalence in these areas is 11.2%, with antidepressant prescribing at 29.4 per 1,000 population — substantially above the London average of 24.1.

narratives.title: Disability and caring burden
narratives.body: London’s disability rate averages 13.2%, but in the most mentally health-deprived neighbourhoods it reaches 19.5%. Unpaid care provision follows a similar pattern: 7.2% of Londoners provide unpaid care overall, rising in areas of highest mental health need. These overlapping burdens suggest that mental health policy cannot be siloed — it intersects with disability support, carer support, and community infrastructure.
